result.markdown: --- title: "Ethics in Data Science" subtitle: "Lecture 11" date: "2026-07-15" format: revealjs: output-file: 11-ethics-slides.html html: output-file: 11-ethics.html auto-stretch: false --- ## Today - Data do not speak for themselves. They require an interpretive lens, not all of which are created equal; - The practice of data science is not ethically neutral or value-free. # Misrepresentation ## Misrepresenting data science results {.smaller} Some common ways people do this, either intentionally or unintentionally, include: ::: incremental - Claiming **causality** where it's not in the scope of inference of the underlying study - Generalizing results to some **broader population** that is not comparable to the underlying study population - Distorting **axes and scales** to make the data tell a different story - Visualizing **spatial areas instead of human density** for issues that depend on and affect humans - Omitting **uncertainty** in reporting ::: ## Recall from day 1... ::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](img/00/personal_inj.png){fig-align="center"} ::: {style="font-size: 0.6em; margin-top: 0.5em;"} Personal Injury Law Firm Blog Post ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](img/00/communicate.png){fig-align="center"} ::: {style="font-size: 0.6em; margin-top: 0.5em;"} Harper and Palayew's (2019) study, "The annual cannabis holiday and fatal traffic crashes", available here. ::: ::: ::: ## Causality - TIME coverage ::: question How plausible is the statement in the title of this article? ::: ![](img/11/exercise-cancer-time.png) ::: aside Alice Park. [Exercise Can Lower Risk of Some Cancers By 20%](https://time.com/4330041/reduce-cancer-risk-exercise/). Time Magazine. 16 May 2016. ::: ## Causality - LA Times coverage ::: question What does "research shows" mean? ::: ![](img/11/exercise-cancer-latimes.png) ::: aside Melissa Healy. [Exercising drives down risk for 13 cancers, research shows](https://www.latimes.com/science/sciencenow/la-sci-sn-exercising-cancer-20160516-story.html).\ Los Angeles Times. 16 May 2016. ::: ## Causality - Original study {.smaller} Moore, Steven C., et al. [**"Association of leisure-time physical activity with risk of 26 types of cancer in 1.44 million adults."**](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamainternalmedicine/fullarticle/2521826) JAMA internal medicine 176.6 (2016): 816-825. - **Volunteers** were **asked** about their physical activity level over the preceding year. - Half exercised less than about 150 minutes per week, half exercised more. - Compared to the bottom 10% of exercisers, the top 10% had lower rates of esophageal, liver, lung, endometrial, colon, and breast cancer. - Researchers found no association between exercising and 13 other cancers (e.g. pancreatic, ovarian, and brain). ::: notes Carl Bergstrom and Jevin West. [Calling Bullshit: The art of skepticism in a data-driven world](https://www.callingbullshit.org/).\ Random House, 2020.\ Sharon Begley. ["Does exercise prevent cancer?"](https://www.statnews.com/2016/05/16/exercise-prevent-cancer/). StatNews. 16 May 2016. ::: ## Axes and scales - Tax cuts {.smaller} ::: question What is the difference between these two pictures? Which presents a better way to represent these data? ::: ![](img/11/axis-start-at-0.png) ::: aside Christopher Ingraham. ["You've been reading charts wrong. Here's how a pro does it."](https://www.washingtonpost.com/business/2019/10/14/youve-been-reading-charts-wrong-heres-how-pro-does-it/). The Washington Post. 14 October 2019. ::: ## Axes and scales - Cost of gas {.smaller} ::: question What is wrong with this picture? How would you correct it? ::: ![](img/11/cost-of-gas.png){fig-align="center" width="70%"} ## Axes and scales - Cost of gas {.scrollable} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} df <- tibble( date = ymd(c("2019-11-01", "2020-10-25", "2020-11-01")), cost = c(3.17, 3.51, 3.57) ) ggplot(df, aes(x = date, y = cost, group = 1)) + geom_point() + geom_line() + geom_label(aes(label = cost), hjust = -0.25) + labs( title = "Cost of gas", subtitle = "National average", x = NULL, y = NULL, caption = "Source: AAA Fuel Gauge Report" ) + scale_x_continuous( breaks = ymd(c("2019-11-01", "2020-10-25", "2020-11-01")), labels = c("Last year", "Last week", "Current"), guide = guide_axis(angle = 90), limits = as.numeric(ymd(c("2019-11-01", "2020-11-29"))), minor_breaks = ymd(c("2019-11-01", "2020-10-25", "2020-11-01")) ) + scale_y_continuous(labels = label_dollar()) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](11-ethics_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ## Axes and scales - COVID in GA {.smaller} ::: question What is wrong with this picture? How would you correct it? ::: ![](img/11/ga-dph-declining-bars.jpg){fig-align="center" width="750"} ::: aside Georgia Department of Public Health. 11 May 2020. ::: ## Axes and scales - COVID in GA ![](img/11/ga-dph-corrected-bars.png){fig-align="center" width="900"} ::: aside Lucy D'Agostino McGowan. [Graph detective](https://livefreeordichotomize.com/posts/2020-05-17-graph-detective/). Live Free or Dichotomize. 17 May 2020. ::: ## Axes and scales - Planned Parenthood services {.smaller} ::: columns ::: {.column width="40%"} ::: question What is wrong with this picture? How would you correct it? ::: ::: ::: {.column width="60%" style="vertical-align: top;"} ![](img/11/pp-misleading.png){fig-align="center" width="80%"} ::: ::: ::: aside Timothy B. Lee. [Whatever you think of Planned Parenthood, this is a terrible and misleading chart](https://www.vox.com/2015/9/29/9417845/planned-parenthood-terrible-chart). Vox. 29 September 2019. ::: ## Axes and scales - Planned Parenthood services {.scrollable} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true"} pp <- tibble( year = c(2006, 2006, 2013, 2013), service = c("Abortion", "Cancer", "Abortion", "Cancer"), n = c(289750, 2007371, 327000, 935573) ) ggplot(pp, aes(x = year, y = n, color = service)) + geom_point(size = 2) + geom_line(linewidth = 1) + geom_text(aes(label = n), nudge_y = 100000) + geom_text( aes(label = year), nudge_y = 200000, color = "darkgray" ) + labs( title = "Services provided by Planned Parenthood", caption = "Source: Planned Parenthood", x = NULL, y = NULL ) + scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2006, 2013)) + scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(big.mark = ",")) + scale_color_manual(values = c("red", "purple")) + annotate( geom = "text", label = "Abortions", x = 2009.5, y = 400000, color = "red" ) + annotate( geom = "text", label = "Cancer screening\nand prevention services", x = 2010.5, y = 1600000, color = "purple" ) + theme_minimal() + theme(legend.position = "none") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](11-ethics_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: ## ## Uncertainty - Catalan independence {.smaller} On December 19, 2014, the front page of Spanish national newspaper El País read *"Catalan public opinion swings toward 'no' for independence, says survey"*. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](11-ethics_files/figure-revealjs/catalan-misleading-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ::: aside Alberto Cairo. [The truthful art: Data, charts, and maps for communication](http://www.thefunctionalart.com/p/the-truthful-art-book.html). New Riders, 2016. ::: ## Uncertainty - Catalan independence {.smaller} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](11-ethics_files/figure-revealjs/catalan-corrected-1.png){width=100%} ::: ::: ::: aside Alberto Cairo. ["Uncertainty and Graphicacy: How Should Statisticians Journalists and Designers Reveal Uncertainty in Graphics for Public Consumption?"](https://ec.europa.eu/eurostat/cros/powerfromstatistics/OR/PfS-OutlookReport-Cairo.pdf), Power from Statistics: Data Information and Knowledge, 2017. ::: # Algorithmic bias ## California Proposition 25 (2020) {.smaller} Popular referendum on 2018's Senate Bill 10: ::: incremental - **YES**: replace cash bail with "risk assessment." - Democratic Party, Governor Gavin Newson, League of Women Voters of California, California Medical Association, Democracy for America (progressive PAC), etc. - **NO**: keep the cash bail system. - Republican Party, American Bail Coalition, ACLU of Southern California, NAACP, California Asian Pacific Chamber of Commerce, etc. - If passed, each county would be empowered to develop a tool that predicts the risk of a suspect reoffending before trial. - Judges would consult this prediction to make bail decisions. ::: ## What might "risk assessment" look like? {.smaller} Something we will study in two weeks: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](11-ethics_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: Let's say above the line means high risk, which means no bail. Is this progress? ## What happens when we try "predictive policing"? {.small .scrollable} 2016 ProPublica article on algorithm used for rating a defendant's risk of future crime: ::: columns ::: {.column width="70%"} > In forecasting who would re-offend, the algorithm made mistakes with black and white defendants at roughly the same rate but in very different ways. > > - The formula was particularly likely to falsely flag black defendants as future criminals, wrongly labeling them this way at almost twice the rate as white defendants. > > - White defendants were mislabeled as low risk more often than black defendants. ::: ::: {.column width="30%"} ![](img/11/machine-bias.png){fig-align="center" width="800"} ::: ::: ::: aside Source: [ProPublica](https://www.propublica.org/article/machine-bias-risk-assessments-in-criminal-sentencing) ::: ## Notice anything? ::: columns ::: {.column width="35%"} ::: question What is common among the defendants who were assigned a high/low risk score for reoffending? ::: ::: ::: {.column width="5%"} ::: ::: {.column width="30%"} ![](img/11/machine-bias-petty-theft-1.png){fig-align="center" width="300" height="250"} ![](img/11/machine-bias-petty-theft-2.png){fig-align="center" width="300" height="180"} ::: ::: {.column width="30%"} ![](img/11/machine-bias-drug-posession-1.png){fig-align="center" width="300" height="250"} ![](img/11/machine-bias-drug-posession-2.png){fig-align="center" width="300" height="180"} ::: ::: ## "But race wasn't in my model" ::: columns ::: {.column width="35%"} ::: question How can an algorithm that doesn't use race as input data be racist? ::: ::: ::: {.column width="5%"} ::: ::: {.column width="60%"} ![](img/11/machine-bias-risk-scores.png){fig-align="center" width="480"} ::: ::: ## Predicting ethnicity {.small .scrollable} [Improving Ecological Inference by Predicting Individual Ethnicity from Voter Registration Record](https://imai.fas.harvard.edu/research/race.html) (Imran and Khan, 2016) > In both political behavior research and voting rights litigation, turnout and vote choice for different racial groups are often inferred using aggregate election results and racial composition. > Over the past several decades, many statistical methods have been proposed to address this ecological inference problem. > We propose an alternative method to reduce aggregation bias by predicting individual-level ethnicity from voter registration records. > Building on the existing methodological literature, we use Bayes's rule to combine the Census Bureau's Surname List with various information from geocoded voter registration records. > We evaluate the performance of the proposed methodology using approximately nine million voter registration records from Florida, where self-reported ethnicity is available. > We find that it is possible to reduce the false positive rate among Black and Latino voters to 6% and 3%, respectively, while maintaining the true positive rate above 80%. > Moreover, we use our predictions to estimate turnout by race and find that our estimates yields substantially less amounts of bias and root mean squared error than standard ecological inference estimates. > We provide open-source software to implement the proposed methodology. > The open-source software is available for implementing the proposed methodology. ## **wru** package The said "source software" is the wru package: . ::: question Do you have any ethical concerns about installing this package? ::: ## **wru** package {.medium} ::: question Was the publication of this model ethical? Does the open-source nature of the code affect your answer? Is it ethical to use this software? Does your answer change depending on the intended use? ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(wru) predict_race(voter.file = voters, surname.only = TRUE) |> select(surname, pred.whi, pred.bla, pred.his, pred.asi, pred.oth) ``` ::: ## **wru** package ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} me <- tibble(surname = "Lim") predict_race(voter.file = me, surname.only = TRUE) ``` ::: ## California Prop 25 did not pass {.smaller} The cash bail system was retained: | Choice | Votes | Percent | |--------|---------------|---------| | Yes | 7,232,380 | 43.59% | | **No** | **9,358,226** | 56.41% | ::: incremental - reasonable people can debate if this outcome is good or bad; - every Californian was invited to decide whether statistics and data science should be deployed to make decisions with major social consequences. They opted out; - This vote was held in the pre-ChatGPT era. What would the outcome be today? Is the case for **YES** stronger or weaker? ::: ## Another algorithmic decision... ![](img/11/lending.png) Armies of stats PhDs go to work on these models. They have no training in the ethics of what they're doing. ## A success story? . . . `Data` + `Model` to predict timing of menstrual cycle: ![](img/11/flo.png){width="24%"} ![](img/11/clue.png){width="24%"} ![](img/11/per_calendar.png){width="24%"} ![](img/11/stardust.png){width="24%"} . . . A perfect microcosm of the themes of our course, and maybe one of the real triumphs of data and modeling improving modern life. . . . # Some good news? ## Faster, more accurate cancer screening? Augmenting doctors' diagnostic capacity so that they make fewer mistakes, treat more people, and focus on other aspects of care: ![](img/11/aamc_cancer.png){fig-align="center" width="700"} ## Recent Nobel Prize ![](img/11/nobel.png){fig-align="center" width="550"} - AlphaFold2: "predicting 3D structures \[of proteins\] ($y$) directly from the primary amino acid sequence ($x$)." - "researchers can now better understand antibiotic resistance and create images of enzymes that can decompose plastic." # Further reading ## How Charts Lie ::: columns ::: column ![](img/11/cairo-how-charts-lie.jpg){width="400"} ::: ::: column [How Charts Lie](http://www.thefunctionalart.com/p/reviews.html) Getting Smarter about Visual Information by Alberto Cairo ::: ::: ## Calling Bullshit ::: columns ::: column ![](img/11/calling-bullshit.png){width="400"} ::: ::: column [Calling Bullshit](https://www.callingbullshit.org/)\ The Art of Skepticism in a\ Data-Driven World by Carl Bergstrom and Jevin West ::: ::: ## Machine Bias ::: columns ::: column ![](img/11/propublica-machine-bias.png){width="400"} ::: ::: column [Machine Bias](https://www.propublica.org/article/machine-bias-risk-assessments-in-criminal-sentencing)\ by Julia Angwin, Jeff Larson, Surya Mattu, and Lauren Kirchner ::: ::: ## Ethics and Data Science ::: columns ::: column ![](img/11/ethics-data-science.jpg){width="400"} ::: ::: column [Ethics and Data Science](https://www.amazon.com/Ethics-Data-Science-Mike-Loukides-ebook/dp/B07GTC8ZN7)\ by Mike Loukides, Hilary Mason, DJ Patil\ (Free Kindle download) ::: ::: ## Weapons of Math Destruction ::: columns ::: column ![](img/11/weapons-of-math-destruction.jpg){width="400"} ::: ::: column [Weapons of Math Destruction](https://www.penguin.co.uk/books/304/304513/weapons-of-math-destruction/9780141985411.html)\ How Big Data Increases Inequality and Threatens Democracy\ by Cathy O'Neil ::: ::: ## Algorithms of Oppression ::: columns ::: column ![](img/11/algorithms-of-oppression.jpg){width="400"} ::: ::: column [Algorithms of Oppression](https://nyupress.org/9781479837243/algorithms-of-oppression/)\ How Search Engines Reinforce Racism\ by Safiya Umoja Noble ::: ::: ## And more recently... [How AI discriminates and what that means for your Google habit](https://newsroom.ucla.edu/stories/how-ai-discriminates-and-what-that-means-for-your-google-habit)\ A conversation with UCLA internet studies scholar Safiya Noble\ by Julia Busiek ## Parting thoughts {.scrollable .smaller} - At some point during your data science learning journey you will learn tools that can be used unethically - You might also be tempted to use your knowledge in a way that is ethically questionable either because of business goals or for the pursuit of further knowledge (or because your boss told you to do so) ::: question How do you train yourself to make the right decisions (or reduce the likelihood of accidentally making the wrong decisions) at those points? ::: # A Brief History of Statistics (Buckle Up) ## Origins - Compared to other sciences, statistics is relatively young: most major contributions happened in the 1900s -Three main waves - Franics Galton and Karl Pearson evolving the field from math - Sir Ronald Fisher and The Design of Experiments - Jerzy Neyman and Egon Pearson ## Galton and Pearson {.smaller} ::: columns ::: column - Galton introduced standard deviation, correlation, regression - Together with Karl Pearson founded mathematical statistics - Other contributions: hypothesis testing, p-values - First statistics department in University College London ::: ::: column ![](img/11/pearsongalton.jpg){width="400"} ::: ::: ## Fisher and The Design of Experiments ::: columns ::: column - Many contributions fundamental to experimental design - Analysis of variance or ANOVA - Agriculture! A very popular data source for statistics ::: ::: column ![](img/11/fisherthefascist.jpeg){width="400"} ::: ::: # Besides statistics, what did these founding father of statistics have in common? ## RACISM! ![](img/11/galtoneugenics.png){width="800"} ![](img/11/galtongeugenics0.png){width="500"} ![](img/11/fishereugenics.png){width="500"} ## Why I bring this up? {.smaller} - Statistics was used to advance eugenics "research" - Acknowledging that statistics is not merely numbers but rather a powerful tool that caused real harm is a starting point toward ethical and thoughtful usage - The midterm tomorrow features the popular (and seemingly innocuous) dataset "Iris": a very famous dataset on flowers - Unfortunately, one of the original uses of that dataset was by Fisher in the Annals of Eugenics - We will use it tomorrow with the understanding that while these people involved in eugenics created many of the methods today, we hold the power to use them ethically and responsibly ## Mathematics Genealogy Project ## Wrapping Everything Up - Data science and statistics contains more power than you think - You have the tools to lead or mislead the public: use your knowledge for good - Be critical about the methods you learn

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
